categories.label: Billing
categories.description: Invoices, payments, refunds, subscription/plan changes, pricing questions.

categories.label: API & Integration
categories.description: API errors, SDK issues, rate limits, auth tokens/keys, webhook/integration failures.

categories.label: Account Access
categories.description: Login failures, password/2FA resets, locked/suspended accounts, permissions/roles.

categories.label: Product Bug
categories.description: Something in the product is broken or behaving incorrectly (not API-specific).

categories.label: Outage
categories.description: Service down, widespread degradation, "everything is broken" reports.

categories.label: Feature Request
categories.description: Asking for new functionality or enhancements.

categories.label: General Inquiry
categories.description: How-to questions, documentation questions, pre-sales questions.

categories.label: Abuse / Policy
categories.description: Reports of misuse, content policy violations, security vulnerability disclosures.

categories.label: Uncategorized
categories.description: Message is too vague/low-info to confidently classify, or the model's response failed validation and the deterministic fallback fired. See 'notes.uncategorized' below - these are two distinct triggers that both land on this label and must be tested separately in the harness.

priority_definitions.High: Active outage, security incident, account lockout blocking business-critical use, data loss, or a billing issue with imminent service interruption. Also: strong anger/urgency signals combined with real business impact.
priority_definitions.Medium: Default level. Bug with a workaround, billing disputes without urgency, degraded (not down) functionality, angry tone without clear business-critical impact.
priority_definitions.Low: Feature requests, general/how-to questions, cosmetic issues, pre-sales inquiries.

edge_case_rules.angry_tone: Raises priority (e.g. Medium -> High). Never changes category - a furious billing complaint is still 'billing', just High priority.
edge_case_rules.short_low_info_message: Model self-selects 'uncategorized' / Tier 1 rather than confidently guessing a category. Priority here is the model's own judgment call for a vague ticket, not the same thing as the system's fallback default (see notes.uncategorized below), which is hardcoded to High regardless of the model's opinion.
edge_case_rules.how_to_phrasing_vs_category: A ticket phrased as a how-to question does not default to 'general_inquiry'. Decide by topic: if it concerns the requester's own account, workspace, data, or a technical constraint/error they're personally hitting, classify by that topic (api_integration, account_access, billing, etc.) even though it reads like a how-to question. Mentioning a specific endpoint, SDK method, or feature name does not by itself decide the category - a question can name a specific mechanism and still be a general, documentation-answerable 'how does this work' question. 'general_inquiry' is reserved for topics that don't match any other category and aren't tied to the requester's own account, workspace, or data.
edge_case_rules.ambiguous_product_bug_vs_api_integration: Decide by where the problem occurs, not by whether API/SDK/webhook words appear in the ticket: if the issue is in how the requester is integrating with the API/SDK programmatically (errors calling it, unexpected responses, webhook/auth failures) -> 'api_integration'; if the issue is in the product's own behavior/UI as experienced by a regular user -> 'product_bug', regardless of whether the ticket happens to mention API, SDK, or an endpoint name. Same principle as how_to_phrasing_vs_category, applied to this pair. Additional tie-break rules should be added here as the harness surfaces new ambiguous pairs.

notes.uncategorized: Two distinct triggers share this label and must NOT be conflated in the harness: (1) model-selected - the ticket is genuinely too vague, and 'uncategorized' is one of the model's allowed output values, chosen deliberately. (2) system-fallback - the model's response failed deterministic validation (bad JSON, category/team/priority not in the allowed lists) after one retry, so the validator overrides the result with the safe default (priority High, see validator.py's FALLBACK_RESULT). The harness needs at least one test ticket that should legitimately produce (1), and a separate test that simulates a malformed model response to confirm (2) actually fires.
